note:
注：我已经更改了本文提到的实际电子邮件公司的名称，原因很简单，因为我对他们的服务不够熟悉，不愿意为他们通过这期杂志鼓吹的所有免费宣传做出贡献。如果您脱离了圈子并想了解更多信息，只需单击文章中的任何一个链接，就不会太难追踪到它。
最近，科技界对一项我将在本文中称为Yo的电子邮件服务议论纷纷，以及由于Yo拒绝使用苹果的应用内购买系统来销售订阅，苹果拒绝使用他们的iOS应用程序。苹果希望Yo通过应用内购买提供订阅的原因是，这将使苹果从所有通过该应用永久订阅的用户的所有订阅费中获得30%的分成(一旦用户订阅超过一年，我认为这一比例可能会降至15%)。显然，Yo更喜欢这些用户通过他们的网站注册，因为这样他们就可以获得100%的订阅费，而苹果什么都得不到。
“由于苹果的市场势力，它收取过高的租金--高速公路抢劫，基本上就是--恐吓人们支付30%，或者拒绝进入他们的市场。”-众议员大卫·西西林(D-RI)在Vergecast上说。
大部分争议都围绕着苹果有选择地执行的政策，即他们是否允许在其应用商店中提供订阅服务的应用，而实际上并不允许用户通过该应用进行订阅。规避这一政策的一个例子是Netflix和Spotify-如果你已经有账户，你可以使用iOS应用程序在iPhone上观看Netflix视频和听Spotify音乐，但如果你没有账户，这些应用程序不仅不给你订阅的选项，而且由于苹果的政策，它们甚至不允许通知用户可以订阅。即使是一条简单的非链接消息“在网络上注册一个账户”也会成为苹果拒绝这款应用的理由。Netflix在几年前移除了这一功能时，对此大加抨击。
Yo的计划似乎是效仿Netflix和Spotify的模式-提供一款应用程序，让现有订户访问他们的电子邮件服务，并简单地避免任何应用内机制(甚至不提这种可能性)，让新用户注册Yo，以避免苹果从订阅费中分成。其想法大概是，用户将自己发现并订阅Yo的服务，然后只有在获得账户后才会安装配套的iOS应用程序。
在这种情况下，苹果没有像Netflix、Spotify和无数其他应用程序那样放过Yo，而是介入并实质上说，“不，他妈的，你要么在你的应用程序中实现应用内订阅，然后给我们30%，要么你去吸鸡蛋。”苹果拒绝了他们应用商店对Yo应用程序的更新，并威胁说，除非Yo遵守，否则将完全删除这款应用程序。
“苹果希望拥有iPhone消费者，这样任何想要接触到这一人群的人都必须支付通行费，并遵守他们的规则。对于一家公司来说，…的权力太大了。“。-马修斯·莫雷拉(Matheusmore Ira)在黑客新闻上。
在我看来，Netflix与Spotify和Yo之间存在差异的原因是非常明显的。苹果的挥手解释与“消费者”应用程序与“商业”应用程序或“阅读器”应用程序与生产力应用程序之间的模糊定义有关，但实际上原因是钱。Netflix是一家大公司，在全球拥有大约2亿订户，其中许多人会在发现自己无法在手机上观看Netflix节目时犹豫不决。Spotify大约有1.5亿订户，他们无疑会认为，无法在他们购买或正在考虑购买的700美元袖珍电脑上听音乐是完全荒谬的。
还有其他反常的例子--Audible应用程序没有应用内订阅机制，Kindle不允许你通过iOS应用程序购买图书，即使是其他订阅的电子邮件应用程序，如Microsoft Outlook，也可以在应用程序商店上使用，而不给苹果公司分一杯羹。但是你有没有注意到那里有一种趋势呢？Netflix、Spotify、亚马逊、微软…。这些公司都有足够的规模和影响力，如果其中任何一家决定从iOS中撤出应用程序，苹果可能会面临严重危险，如果他们决定从法律角度挑战苹果的做法，潜在的麻烦甚至会更多。
“这让我莫名其妙。我可以买一台USD1k+设备，但仍然不能在上面安装我想要的应用程序，因为制造商不想让我这样做，这是合法的吗？真是胡说八道。“--”黑客新闻“中的Not Robot。
如果苹果继续始终如一地坚持他们的政策，要求终身削减通过iOS应用程序访问的所有订阅服务，并禁止在他们的设备上使用Netflix、Spotify、Audible和Outlook，这显然会让苹果用户和这些设备的销售蒙受损失。这些服务足够大，是如此多用户移动生活中不可或缺的一部分，以至于人们会成群结队地转而使用Android来取回它们。但对于像Yo这样刚刚推出的全新电子邮件服务来说，苹果非常乐意拿到这笔钱-没有人会因为一项他们从未听说过的新电子邮件服务而哭泣，对吗？
不同的是，在这种情况下，新的电子邮件服务是由一家公司创建的，该公司也为远程员工提供久负盛名的非常受欢迎的协作服务。虽然就用户数量而言，这项服务可能无法与Netflix或Spotify相提并论，但这项服务在极其懂技术的人群中非常受欢迎-这正是苹果可能应该避免激怒的那类人，因为当某事引起他们的愤怒时，他们非常善于将其提升到一般的在线意识中。
我想在这里简短地指出苹果的一个有趣的虚伪之处。对于Android应用商店上发布的应用，谷歌与苹果有类似的模式--他们从每个用户订阅的第一年抽取30%的提成，之后永久收取15%的提成，不同之处在于他们不会禁止实施替代支付方式的应用。当你发布一款Android应用程序时，你可以自由选择是使用谷歌的订阅机制并向他们支付分成，还是实施自己的解决方案而不向谷歌支付任何费用。我让你猜一猜，当苹果在Android版本的Apple Music应用中实现应用内订阅机制时，选择了哪一个。
如果苹果的行为如此不合理，以荒谬的反消费者理由拒绝应用程序，那么为什么我们-既有开发人员，通过向他们的生态系统提供新的应用程序来支撑他们的行为，也有消费者，他们通过继续购买价格过高的硬件来支撑他们的行为-为什么还要继续支持他们呢？
对于开发者来说，这个问题的答案似乎和苹果允许Netflix和Spotify保留在iPhone上的原因一样显而易见，尽管这违反了应用商店的政策。这一切都是为了钱。开发者想要接触到已经存在的消费者基础，这些消费者显然有钱消费，因为他们购买了市场上最昂贵的手机之一，但他们想要免费的访问权限(或者至少是以比苹果认为的价值更低的价格)。
“30%看起来确实有点高。苹果收取这么高的费用有什么理由？“--Joegibbs对黑客新闻的采访
虽然我完全同意苹果的政策和做法是骇人听闻的(如果我对现实世界中的苹果一无所知，并且正在读一个虚构的故事，里面有一家敌对的公司像苹果那样行事，我想我可能会认为这是荒谬的不切实际的)，但我也认为，苹果显然已经获得了成为邪恶混蛋的权利。他们打造了人们喜爱的产品，通过对媒体的精心控制培育了自己的品牌，并将他们的公司推上了巅峰。在我看来，他们拥有“垄断”的想法显然是荒谬的。垄断什么？他们自己品牌的计算设备，而他们是这些设备的独家制造商？他们是哪种操作系统的唯一开发者和维护者？如果你不喜欢苹果，并不是没有其他设备和平台可供选择。
比方说，我们通过法律手段摧毁苹果公司--我们向他们传达的信息是，因为他们太成功了，他们不再有权在他们的设备上允许哪些应用程序，以及他们可以向开发者收取多少钱来访问他们的平台，来决定自己的条款。只要想一想，这种先例可能会被滥用于其他公司的所有方式。
让我们假设，Yo电子邮件服务通过出色的营销和令人惊叹的产品，设法扩大了他们的用户基础，并将电子邮件市场占领到了闻所未闻的水平。那些家伙让Gmail看起来像一家夫妻店。现在，假设他们决定提供一项新服务，允许内容提供商接触到Yo的订阅者-就像时事通讯之类的东西。他们是否应该被允许规定内容提供商必须支付多少费用才能在其中一份时事通讯上发布内容？或者，他们是不是太大了，以至于现在“垄断”了自己的YO时事通讯，不得不像苹果一样被拿下？我们真的应该因为公司自身的成功而惩罚它们吗？谁来决定什么时候发生呢？多大才算太大？
我不知道，对我来说哭垄断似乎很愚蠢。解决方案是显而易见的。停止为iOS开发应用程序。当然，如果你不能接触到这些消费者，你会受到巨大的打击，但如果你的公司或产品的结构是这样的，如果没有能力吸吮苹果的乳头，它就无法生存，那么你做出了错误的决定，在我看来，无论苹果决定为此向你铲出什么狗屎，你都是罪有应得。在这一点上，我们已经知道，苹果是一只反复无常的贪财野兽，它可以而且确实会摧毁整个企业，这些企业犯下了将成功寄托在苹果平台上的错误，这是一个你要么必须知情地承担风险，要么在理想情况下通过对苹果竖起中指来完全避免的风险。
这并不一定意味着让你的产品或服务在iPhone上完全不可用-你总是可以编写一个移动友好的网络应用程序。也就是说，直到苹果决定在Safari中屏蔽或破坏你的应用程序，从技术上讲，Safari是iPhone上唯一允许的Web浏览器(其他浏览器也可以使用，但由于更严厉的应用程序商店政策，它们必须使用与Safari相同的渲染引擎，这使得苹果能够完全控制这些浏览器在幕后的实际运行方式)。
别再买iPhone了。我是认真的。它们定价过高，被苹果锁定，并以令人愤怒的方式控制着，伤害了你，即使苹果成功地让你对此一无所知。不要在你买Mac的时候买--虽然它们没有iPhone那么严格，但很明显，苹果希望它们这样做，而且它们很快就会走上同样有害于消费者的道路。
我知道这些设备很漂亮，手感也很好，也许你的朋友都有一个，而你想要发出你是人群中的一员的信号，但这不值得。苹果应该为他们的可怕行为受到惩罚，而对我们来说，最好的办法就是用我们的钱包。买一个安卓系统吧，市面上有一些和iPhone一样漂亮的好手机，而且它们的价格也往往要低得多。或者去买一部翻盖手机，我不知道，只要不再把钱给苹果就行了。
事实上，我知道这些解决方案都不会真正实现。资本主义应该是这样运作的--伤害消费者的公司不应该成功，而迎合消费者的竞争应该成功。但开发者将继续追逐苹果应用商店的金矿，消费者将继续以两倍于旧款的价格购买最新款的iPhone，因为这就是资本主义的真正运作方式。已经掌握了所有权力和资金的公司有办法继续保持这一地位，甚至在竞争开始之前就扼杀了任何可能会推翻它们的竞争。
也许最终的解决方案是反垄断法律解决方案。也许公司不应该被允许像苹果一样成功，因为它对我们所有人都造成了集体伤害。在一个理想的世界里，人们会意识到他们正在受到伤害，并惩罚肇事者，但我们并不生活在一个理想的世界里。在苹果的案例中，消费者在很大程度上没有意识到，坦率地说，只要他们有闪闪发光的新玩具，他们就不在乎。开发人员更清楚，也更喜欢抱怨和抱怨，但也不是真的在乎，因为等待他们的潜在回报是，如果他们能够通过苹果的审查程序，进入现有的客户群，比试图从零开始建立客户基础简单得多。
我对这样的法律行动是否会成功的希望并不是特别高。特别是在美国，贿赂政客基本上是通过游说合法化的，而苹果公司有这么多淫秽的钱可以扔给他们。
我上小学时的第一台电脑是Macintosh Classic，我从小就和Mac一起长大，并且一直拥有PowerMacG4系列，所以写这一切让我有点痛苦。今天我一点也不支持苹果。我的手机是Android(尽管我也在考虑替代它)，我的电脑都是运行Linux的非Mac电脑，我已经十多年没有为任何苹果平台开发软件了。压垮我骆驼背的那根稻草很久以前就来了。我敦促每个人问问你们自己，你们的极限在哪里。苹果有多糟糕，你才会停止购买iPhone或开发iOS应用程序？你愿意给予他们多少控制权来控制你如何使用和访问你花了这么多钱购买的设备？
note_en:
Note: I have changed the name of the actual email company referred to by this story, simply for the reason that I’m not familiar enough with their service to feel comfortable contributing to all the free publicity they’re drumming up through this issue. If you’re out of the loop and want to learn more just click any of the links in the article and it won’t be too hard to track down.
  There has been a lot of hubbub in the tech sphere lately over an email service I will be calling Yo in this article, and  Apple’s rejection of their iOS app due to Yo’s refusal to use Apple’s in-app purchase system to sell subscriptions. The reason Apple wants Yo to offer subscriptions via an in-app purchase is because that would give Apple a 30% cut of all subscription fees for all users who subscribe through the app in perpetuity (once a user has subscribed for over a year I think it may drop to 15%). Obviously Yo would prefer those users to sign up via their website instead, because then they get 100% of the subscription fees and Apple gets nothing.
 “Because of the market power that Apple has, it is charging exorbitant rents — highway robbery, basically — bullying people to pay 30 percent or denying access to their market.” –Rep. David Cicilline (D-RI) on  The Vergecast
 Much of the controversy lies around Apple’s selectively enforced policies around whether they allow apps in their app store that provide access to a subscription service without actually allowing users to subscribe through the app. An example of apps that skirt this policy are Netflix and Spotify–if you already have accounts you can use the iOS app to watch Netflix videos and listen to Spotify music on your iPhone, but if you don’t have an account not only do those apps not give you the option to subscribe, due to Apple’s policies they’re not even allowed to inform users that it’s possible to subscribe at all. Even a simple non-link message stating “Sign up for an account on the web” would be grounds for Apple to reject the app. Netflix made a  big stink about this when they removed the functionality a couple years ago.
 Yo’s plan seems to have been to follow the Netflix and Spotify model–provide an app that gives existing subscribers access to their email services, and simply eschew any in-app mechanism (or even mention of the possibility) for new users to sign up for Yo in order to avoid Apple skimming their cut from the subscription fees. The idea would presumably be that users will discover and subscribe to Yo’s services on their own and then install the companion iOS app only after they have already obtained an account.
 In this case, instead of giving Yo a pass as they did Netflix and Spotify and countless other apps, Apple stepped in and essentially said “Nah, fuck that, you either implement in-app subscriptions in your app and give us our 30% or you can go suck an egg,” and rejected an update to Yo’s app from their app store and have threatened to remove the app entirely unless Yo complies.
 “Apple wants to own iPhone consumers so that anyone who wants access to that demographic must pay the toll and follow their rules. It’s far too much power for one corporation…” –matheusmoreira on  Hacker News
  The reason for the discrepancy between Netflix and Spotify and Yo is, to me, perfectly obvious. Apple’s hand-wavy explanation has to do with nebulous definitions of “consumer” apps versus “commercial” apps or “reader” apps versus productivity apps, but in actuality the reason is money. Netflix is a huge company with somewhere in the neighborhood of 200 million subscribers world-wide, many of whom would balk at discovering they can’t watch Netflix shows on their phone. Spotify has somewhere around 150 million subscribers who would undoubtedly consider the inability to listen to their music on the $700 pocket-computer they either bought or are thinking about buying to be completely ridiculous.
 There are other anomalous examples as well–The Audible app has no in-app subscription mechanism, Kindle doesn’t let you buy books through the iOS app, even other subscription-based email apps like Microsoft Outlook are permitted on the app store without giving Apple a cut. But are you noticing a trend there? Netflix, Spotify, Amazon, Microsoft… these are all companies with enough size and clout that Apple could be in serious danger if any of them decided to pull their apps from iOS, and even more potential trouble if they decided to challenge Apple’s practices from a legal standpoint.
 “It’s baffling to me. I can buy a USD1k+ device and still not be able to install on it the apps I want because the manufacturer doesn’t want me to, and this is legal? Such horsecrap.” –notRobot on  Hacker News
 If Apple remained consistent and diligent in their policy to demand a lifetime cut of all subscription services accessed via iOS apps and banned Netflix, Spotify, Audible, and Outlook from their devices, it would clearly cost Apple users and sales of those devices. Those services are big enough and an integral part of so many users’ mobile lives that people would switch to Android in droves to get them back. But for something like Yo, a brand-spanking new email service that just launched, Apple is more than happy to rake them over the coals in an attempt to get that money–nobody is going to cry over a new email service they’ve never heard of, right?
 Except that in this case the new email service was founded by a company that also offers a well-established and very popular collaboration service for remote workers. While probably not in the same ballpark as Netflix or Spotify as far as subscriber count goes, this service is much-beloved among the extremely technically-literate–exactly the kind of people that Apple should probably avoid pissing off because when something catches their ire they’re pretty good at elevating it into general online consciousness.
 I would like to take a brief moment here to point out an amusing hypocrisy on Apple’s part. Google has a similar model to Apple for apps released on the Android app store–they take a 30% cut of the first year of every user’s subscription and then 15% after that in perpetuity, with the difference being that they do not ban apps for implementing alternate payment methods. When you publish an Android app you can freely choose between using Google’s subscription mechanism and paying them a cut, or implementing your own solution and not paying Google anything. I’ll give you one guess which one Apple has chosen when implementing the in-app subscription mechanism in the Android version of their Apple Music app.
  If Apple is behaving so unreasonably by rejecting apps for ridiculous anti-consumer reasons, why are we–both developers, who prop up their behavior by feeding their ecosystem with new apps; and consumers, who prop up their behavior by continuing to buy their overpriced hardware–continuing to support them?
 The answer to that for developers seems just as obvious as Apple’s reasons for allowing Netflix and Spotify to remain on iPhones despite being in violation of app store policies. It’s all about money. Developers want access to that pre-existing user base of consumers who clearly have money to spend because they bought one of the most expensive phones on the market, but they want that access for free (or at least for cheaper than Apple has deemed it’s worth).
 “30% does seem a little bit high. What justification does Apple use for charging that much?” –joegibbs on  Hacker News
 While I am in complete agreement that Apple’s policies and practices are egregious (if I had no knowledge of Apple in the real world and was reading a fictional story in which an antagonistic company behaved the way Apple does, I think I would probably discard it as absurdly unrealistic), I am also of the opinion that Apple has clearly earned the right to be evil assholes. They built products that people love, cultivated their brand through careful control of the media, and vaulted their company into the stratosphere. The idea that they have a “monopoly” seems patently ridiculous to me. A monopoly over what? Their own brand of computing devices that they are the sole manufacturers of? Operating systems for which they are the sole developers and maintainers? It’s not like there aren’t other devices and platforms to choose from if you don’t like Apple.
 Let’s say we tear down Apple through legal means–we send them the message that because they’re so successful they no longer have a right to dictate their own terms around what apps they allow on their devices and how much money they can charge developers to have access to their platform.  Just think of all the ways that precedent could be abused against other companies.
 Let’s say, hypothetically, that the Yo email service, through brilliant marketing and an amazing product, manages to grow their user base and capture the email market to unheard of levels. Those dudes make Gmail look like a Ma and Pa shop. Now let’s say they decide to offer a new service that allows content providers to reach Yo’s subscribers–like a newsletter or something. Should they be allowed to dictate how much a content provider must pay to have content in one of those newsletters? Or are they so big that they now have a “monopoly” over their own Yo newsletter and have to be taken down like Apple? Should we really be punishing companies for their own success? Who decides when that happens? How big is too big?
  I don’t know, it seems silly to me to cry about monopolies. The solution is obvious. Stop making apps for iOS. Sure, you’re going to take a huge hit without access to those consumers, but if your company or product is structured in such a way that it can’t survive without the ability to suckle at Apple’s teat then you’ve made bad decisions and in my opinion deserve whatever shit Apple decides to shovel at you because of it. By this point we already know that Apple is a fickle money-hungry beast who can and does destroy entire businesses that have made the mistake of hinging their success on Apple’s platform, and that’s a risk you either have to knowingly take or, ideally, just avoid entirely by giving Apple the middle finger.
 That doesn’t necessarily mean making your product or service completely unavailable on the iPhone–you can always write a mobile-friendly web app. Until, that is, Apple decides to block or break your app in Safari, which is technically the only web browser allowed on the iPhone (other browsers are available but due to more draconian app store policies they must use the same rendering engine that Safari uses, which gives Apple the same full control over how those browsers actually function under the hood).
  Stop buying iPhones. Seriously. They’re overpriced and locked-down and controlled by Apple in infuriating ways that hurt you, even if Apple has succeeded in keeping you ignorant of that fact. Don’t buy Macs while you’re at it–while they’re not quite as restrictive as iPhones, it’s very clear that Apple wants them to be and they are quickly headed down the same consumer-harmful road.
 I know that the devices are pretty and feel nice and maybe your friends all have one and you want to signal that you’re one of the crowd, but it’s not worth it. Apple should be punished for their horrendous behavior and the best way for us to do that is with our wallets. Get an Android, there are some nice ones out there just as pretty as iPhones and they tend to cost quite a bit less too. Or go full hipster and get a flip phone, I don’t know, just stop giving your money to Apple.
  In reality, I know none of these solutions are actually going to come to fruition. It’s how capitalism is  supposed to work–companies that hurt consumers should not succeed while the competition that caters to consumers should succeed. But developers are going to keep chasing that Apple app store gold mine and consumers will keep buying the latest iPhone model at twice the price of their old one because that’s how capitalism really works. Companies that already hold all the power and money have the means to remain in that position and stifle any competition that could potentially topple them before it even gets off the ground.
 Maybe the ultimate solution is an anti-trust legal one. Maybe companies shouldn’t be allowed to be as successful as Apple because of how it collectively hurts us all. In an ideal world people would recognize that they’re being hurt and punish the perpetrators, but we don’t live in an ideal world. In the case of Apple, consumers are largely unaware and frankly don’t care as long as they have their shiny new toys. Developers are more aware and like to bitch and moan but also don’t  really care because the potential payoff waiting for them if they can slip through Apple’s review process and gain access to that existing customer base is much simpler than trying to build a customer base from scratch.
 My hopes for such legal action ever succeeding are not particularly high. Especially in the USA where bribing politicians is essentially legalized through lobbying and Apple has such obscene amounts of money to toss their way.
 My first computer in grade school was a Macintosh Classic and I grew up with and owned Macs all the way through the PowerMac G4 line, so it pains me a bit to write all of this. Today I don’t support Apple at all. My phone is an Android (although I am considering alternatives to that as well) and my computers are all non-Macs running Linux and I haven’t developed software for any Apple platform in over a decade. The straw that broke my camel’s back came a long time ago. I urge everyone to ask yourselves where your limit is. How bad does Apple have to be before you’ll stop buying iPhones or developing iOS apps? How much control are you willing to give them over how you can use and access the devices that you pay so much money for?
title: 别再抱怨苹果了，别再用它们了
title_en: Quit whining about Apple and just stop using them

note:
此举是德国政府应对右翼极端主义和仇恨犯罪上升的更广泛努力的一部分-它将右翼极端主义和仇恨犯罪与网上仇恨言论的传播联系在一起。
德国现有的“网络执法法”(也就是NetzDG法)于2017年在该国生效，规定社交网络平台有义务在设定的最后期限内远程发表仇恨言论，对于简单的案件，最高可达24小时-如果他们不遵守，将被处以最高5000万欧元的罚款。
昨天，议会通过了一项改革，扩大了NetzDG的范围，在平台上设定了报告义务，要求他们向联邦刑事警察办公室报告某些类型的“犯罪内容”。
NetzDG法律的更广泛改革仍在并行进行，旨在增强用户权利和透明度，包括简化用户通知，使人们更容易反对内容删除，并已成功上诉恢复的内容，以及其他调整。对于平台来说，更广泛的透明度报告要求也迫在眉睫。
NetzDG的法律一直存在争议，批评人士从一开始就警告称，它将激励平台删除内容，而不是冒着罚款的风险，从而导致对言论自由的限制。(也就是“过度屏蔽”的风险。)。2018年，人权观察(Human Rights Watch)称其为一部有缺陷的法律，批评它“含糊、过于宽泛，并将私营企业变成过于热心的审查机构，以避免巨额罚款，使用户没有司法监督或上诉的权利”。
仇恨言论规则的最新变化也同样具有争议性：现在人们担心的是，社交媒体巨头在没有强有力的法律理由的情况下，被拉拢来帮助国家建立关于公民的大规模数据库。
1.数据保护问题。所有被标记的内容以及受影响用户的各种个人数据都会流入执法机构的一个庞大的中央数据库。
对最新法律改革的一些修正案被否决，其中包括绿党提交的一项修正案，该修正案将防止报告的社交媒体帖子的作者的个人数据自动发送给警方。
该政党担心新的报告义务被滥用的风险-导致实际上没有发布任何犯罪内容的公民的数据最终被警方举报。
它还辩称，在其他各种批评中，只有很弱的通知要求通知被标记帖子的作者，他们的数据已经传递给警方。
该党曾提议，只有帖子的内容将直接传输给警方，如果确实需要调查某一特定内容，警方将能够从该平台请求相关的个人数据。
德国政府对仇恨言论法进行改革之前，2019年亲难民的政客沃尔特·吕布克(Walter Lübcke)被新纳粹分子谋杀-德国政府表示，在此之前，德国政府在网上进行了有针对性的威胁和仇恨言论。
本月早些时候，警方突袭了多个州的40名仇恨言论嫌疑人，他们被控根据国家媒体发布关于吕布克的“刑事相关评论”。
政府还辩称，网上仇恨言论对言论自由产生了寒蝉效应，通过恐吓目标人群对民主产生了有害影响-这意味着他们无法在没有恐惧的情况下自由表达自己或参与社会。
多年来，在科技公司签署了自愿的欧盟仇恨言论行为准则后，在泛欧盟层面，欧盟委员会(European Commission)一直在向平台施压，要求其改善有关仇恨言论取缔的报道。
它现在还在就平台规则和治理的更广泛变化进行咨询--根据即将出台的“数字服务法案”(Digital Services Act)，该法案将考虑科技巨头应该为他们围栏的内容承担多少责任。
note_en:
The move is part of a wider push by the German government to tackle a rise in right wing extremism and hate crime — which it links to the spread of hate speech online.
 Germany’s existing Network Enforcement Act (aka the NetzDG law) came into force in the country in  2017, putting an obligation on social network platforms to remote hate speech within set deadlines as tight as 24 hours for easy cases — with fines of up to €50M should they fail to comply.
  Yesterday the parliament  passed a reform which extends NetzDG by placing a reporting obligation on platforms which requires them to report certain types of “criminal content” to the Federal Criminal Police Office.
 A  wider reform of the NetzDG law remains ongoing in parallel, that’s intended to bolster user rights and transparency, including by simplifying user notifications and making it easier for people to object to content removals and have successfully appealed content restored, among other tweaks. Broader transparency reporting requirements are also looming for platforms.
 The NetzDG law has always been controversial, with critics warning from the get go that it would lead to restrictions on freedom of expression by incentivizing platforms to remove content rather than risk a fine. (Aka, the risk of ‘overblocking’.)  In 2018  Human Rights Watch dubbed it a flawed law — critiquing it for being “vague, overbroad, and turn[ing] private companies into overzealous censors to avoid steep fines, leaving users with no judicial oversight or right to appeal”.
 The latest change to hate speech rules is no less controversial: Now the concern is that social media giants are being co-opted to help the state build massive databases  on citizens without robust legal justification.
 1. Data protection concerns. All flagged content and with it all kinds of personal data of the affected users flow into one giant central data base of a law enforcement agency
 A number of amendments to the latest legal reform were rejected, including one tabled by the Greens which would have prevented the personal data of the authors of reported social media posts from being automatically sent to the police.
 The political party is concerned about the risk of the new reporting obligation being abused — resulting in data on citizens who have not in fact posted any criminal content ending up with the police.
 It also argues there are only weak notification requirements to inform authors of flagged posts that their data has been passed to the police, among sundry other criticisms.
 The party had proposed that only the post’s content would be transmitted directly to police who would have been able to request associated personal data from the platform should there be a genuine need to investigate a particular piece of content.
 The German government’s reform of hate speech law follows the 2019 murder of a pro-refugee politician, Walter Lübcke, by neo nazis — which it said was preceded by targeted threats and hate speech online.
 Earlier this month police staged raids on 40 hate speech suspects across a number of states who are accused of posting “criminally relevant comments” about Lübcke, per  national media.
 The  government also argues that hate speech online has a chilling effect on free speech and a deleterious impact on democracy by intimidating those it targets — meaning they’re unable to freely express themselves or participate without fear in society.
 At the pan-EU level, the European Commission has been  pressing platforms to improve their reporting around hate speech takedowns for a number of years, after tech firms signed up to voluntary  EU Code of Conduct on hate speech.
 It is also now  consulting on wider changes to platform rules and governance — under a forthcoming Digital Services Act which will consider how much liability tech giants should face for content they’re fencing.
title: 德国收紧在线仇恨言论规则，要求平台向联邦政府发送报告
title_en: Germany tightens online hate speech rules to make platforms send reports to feds

note:
编者按：这是我们研究风险投资多样性系列的第三篇。在这里阅读第一个故事，在这里阅读我们的第二个故事，并注册我们的时事通讯以获得最新的更新。
尽管在乔治·弗洛伊德(George Floyd)遇害后，社交媒体上以及其他地方都在善意地谈论多元化和包容性的必要性，但在风险投资领域，有一件事比其他任何事情都更能说明问题：金钱。
当然，风投和创始人可以做相当多的事情来改善多样性，但业内人士表示，为整个生态系统提供资金的有限合伙人才能发挥最大的作用。
罗伯特·托伊戈基金会(Robert Toigo Foundation)联合创始人、Fitzgibbon Toigo&amp；Co董事长苏·托伊戈(Sue Toigo；)表示，只有有限合伙人才有权力，可以要求他们应该或不应该与谁一起投资。
在这个公众意识提高的时刻，迫使大型公共养老基金承诺将更多的美元投入少数族裔控制的基金，可能会产生重大影响。与他们的企业亲属不同，这些公共机构代表着这个国家广泛而多样化的地区，为教师、消防员和市政工人等公务员做出投资决策。
两大基金，CalPERS和伊利诺伊市退休基金，说明了管理养老金的规则和管理养老金的人之间的截然不同。
2014年，美国国家风险投资协会(National Venture Capital Association)首届多元化和包容性委员会联合主席、Scale Venture Partners联合创始人凯特·米切尔(Kate Mitchell)表示：我们的资金来源是有限合伙人--比如洛杉矶市、加州大学洛杉矶分校(CalPERS)、基金会和捐赠基金。他们的成员多种多样，他们非常关心这一点。
根据PledgeLA的数据，只有2%的风投投资伙伴认为自己是非裔美国人或拉丁裔，风投投资的公司中只有不到10%的公司是由女性或有色人种领导的。哈佛商学院(Harvard Business School)研究金融人口统计学的教授保罗·A·冈珀斯(Paul A.Gompers)表示，有限合伙人可能会大大增加这两个数字。
冈珀斯说，你投资的人看起来很像你。我们知道有一些服务不足的企业家，这些机会或许可以创造更大的回报。
作为对#MeToo运动的回应，养老基金、基金会和主权财富基金的行业代言人机构有限合作伙伴协会(Institution Limited Partners Association)在2018年扩大了其尽职调查问卷，以衡量种族和性别多样性以及招聘和晋升。米切尔说，这些变化已经产生了明显的影响，提高了公司的门槛，这些公司现在必须回答有关招聘和多样性的问题。
她说，这不是一天就能完成的。如果你要实现它，你必须付出持续的努力。
养老基金的鼻祖，加州公共雇员退休制度，于1986年从种族隔离的南非剥离，并于2000年抛售烟草库存。它也不会投资于热力煤矿、在加州被禁止生产枪支的制造商，以及在苏丹和伊朗经营的企业。但事实证明，区分不同资金的优先顺序更加棘手，因为1996年选民支持的209号提案，CalPERS表示，该提案禁止它基于种族、民族或性别给予优惠。
东洛杉矶资本(East Los Capital)联合创始人伊曼纽尔·普莱特兹(Emanuel Pleitez)表示，有限合伙人喜欢把209号提案作为他们什么都做不了的原因。普莱特兹主持着一个年度会议，旨在教育担任机构董事会成员的官员如何投资资产类别。但你仍然可以收集数据，保持透明度，并确保公众知道你在做什么。有限合伙人有一个巨大的机会来做他们的工作，那就是挑选最好的经理和多样化的供应商。
Pleitez认为，由于不强调多样性，公共机构将财富规模向获得了超额利润的白人资产管理公司倾斜。
为了合法地促进多元化，管理着约4000亿美元资产的CalPERS在1991年启动了一个新兴经理人项目。但去年，它将这个已经很小的计划从35亿美元削减到了仅5亿美元的管理资产。
CalPERS拒绝让任何人接受采访，一位女发言人解释说，下调收费是在更广泛的努力下进行的，目的是通过转向内部管理95%的私募股权投资来降低费用和提高回报，此前这一比例为80%。(CalPERS表示，在上一财年，它还聘请了700家公司，以鼓励董事会成员更加多元化，其中一半这样做了。)
CIO网站获得的一份内部备忘录警告称，终止该项目可能会受到媒体或立法机构的关注，但表示由于长期表现不佳，削减是必要的。
托伊戈说，很多女性和少数族裔基金实际上已经关闭。所有数据都表明，当你拥有不同的董事会和不同的领导层时，你会获得更好的回报。我会争辩说，你没有注意数据，实际上违反了你的受托责任。托戈说：除非你只卖给白人，否则如果我在一家公司工作，我会希望投资过程中的每个观点都能体现出来。
管理着448亿美元资产的伊利诺伊州市政退休基金(IMRF)采取了与CalPERS&39；相反的做法，高度强调投资决策的多样性。
IMRF首席投资官迪瓦尼·沙阿(Dhvani Shah)表示，这不是关于你是否能做到多元化或满足回报的问题。我们两者都能做到。
截至去年年底，少数族裔管理的资产占IMRF积极管理资产的33.8%，较2018年增长19.6%。
沙阿说，根据伊利诺伊州养老金法典，她的任务是优先考虑多样性，该法典规定，养老基金应该在财务和信托审慎的范围内，在最大程度上可行地增加受托人的种族、民族和性别多样性。

note_en:
Editor's note: This is the third in our series examining diversity in venture capital. Read  the first story here,  our second one here and  sign up for our newsletter to get the latest updates.
 For all the well-intentioned talk on social media and beyond about the need for diversity and inclusion after the killing of George Floyd, there is one thing that speaks louder than anything else in venture capital: Money.
 There is certainly a considerable amount that VCs and founders can do to improve diversity, but those in the industry say it is the limited partners who fund the whole ecosystem who could make the biggest difference.
 "It's the LPs that have the power and can demand who they should or shouldn't be investing with," said Sue Toigo, co-founder of the Robert Toigo Foundation and chair of Fitzgibbon Toigo & Co.
 At this moment of heightened public awareness, forcing big public pension funds to commit to putting more of their dollars in funds controlled by minorities could have a major impact. These public institutions, unlike their corporate kin, represent a wide and diverse swath of the country, making investment decisions for public servants like teachers, firefighters and municipal workers.
 Two major funds, CALPERS and the Illinois Municipal Retirement Fund, illustrate the stark differences in the rules that govern pensions and the people who manage them.
 "Our sources of capital are the LPs – like the city of L.A., CalPERS, foundations, and endowments," said Kate Mitchell, who co-chaired the National Venture Capital Association's first diversity and inclusion committee in 2014 and co-founder of Scale Venture Partners. "Their constituents are diverse, and they care greatly about this."
 Just 2% of VC investment partners identify as African American or Latino and less than 10% of VC-funded companies are led by women or people of color,  according to PledgeLA. LPs could greatly increase both numbers, according to Paul A. Gompers, a professor at Harvard Business School who studies the demographics of finance.
 "Who you invest in looks a lot like who you are," Gompers said. "We know there are underserved pockets of entrepreneurs out there and those opportunities could perhaps create greater returns."
 In response to the #MeToo movement, the Institutional Limited Partners Association, the industry voice for pension funds, foundations and sovereign wealth funds, expanded its due diligence questionnaire in 2018 to measure ethnic and gender diversity as well as hiring and promotion. Those changes, Mitchell said, have had a noticeable impact raising the bar with firms that must now answer to questions about hiring and diversity.
 "It isn't done in a day," she said. "If you are going to make it happen, you have to make it a sustained effort."
     The granddaddy of pension funds,  California Public Employees' Retirement System, divested from apartheid South Africa in 1986 and got out of tobacco stocks in 2000. It also doesn't invest in thermal coal miners, manufacturers that make guns banned in California and businesses operating in Sudan and Iran. But prioritizing diverse funds has proven trickier because of the 1996 voter-backed  Proposition 209, which CalPERS says bars it from giving preferential based on race, ethnicity or gender.
 "LPs love to use Proposition 209 as the reason why they can't do anything," said Emanuel Pleitez, co-founder of East Los Capital who runs an annual conference aimed at educating officials that sit on institutional boards about how to invest in asset classes. "But you can still gather data and be transparent and be sure that the public knows what you are doing. There's a massive opening for LPs to do their jobs that's about picking the best managers and vendors that are diverse."
 Pleitez argues that by not emphasizing diversity, public institutions have tipped the scale of wealth toward a class of white asset managers who have gained outsized profits.
 In an effort to legally promote diversity, CalPERS, which manages about $400 billion in assets, started an "emerging manager" program in 1991. But  last year it slashed the already small program from $3.5 billion to just $500 million in assets under management.
 CalPERS declined to make anyone available for an interview., A spokeswoman explained the reduction occurred amid a broader effort to reduce fees and increase returns by shifting to managing 95% of private equity investments internally, up from 80% before. (CALPERS says during the last fiscal year it also "engaged" 700 companies to encourage greater diversity on their boards, half of which did so.)
 An internal memo obtained by the website CIO warned that terminating the program "could receive media or legislative attention" but said the cuts were necessary because of long-term underperformance.
 "A lot of the women and minority funds have actually closed," said Toigo. "All of the data indicates when you have diverse boards and diverse leadership you have better returns. I would argue you're actually violating your fiduciary responsibility by not paying attention to the data. Unless you're only selling to white people, if I was at a firm I would want every point of view represented in the investment process," said Toigo.
 The Illinois Municipal Retirement Fund (IMRF), with $44.8 billion in assets under management, has taken the opposite approach of CalPERS', placing a high emphasis on diversity in its investment decisions.
 "It's not about whether you can just do diversity or meet returns," said Dhvani Shah, IMRF's Chief Investment Officer. "We can do both."
 As of the end of last year, minorities managed 33.8% of IMRF's actively managed assets, a 19.6% increase from 2018.
 Shah says she is mandated to prioritize diversity under the  Illinois Pension Code, which states that pension funds should "increase the racial, ethnic, and gender diversity of its fiduciaries, to the greatest extent feasible within the bounds of financial and fiduciary prudence."
    Editor's note: This is the third in our series examining diversity in venture capital. Read  the first story here,  our second one here and  sign up for our newsletter to get the latest updates.
title: 想要解决风险投资的多元化问题吗？从养老基金开始
title_en: Want to Solve Venture Capital's Diversity Problem? Start with Pension Funds

note:
传说中的“Linux桌面年”从未实现过，也可能永远不会实现。这是否意味着台式PC上的Linux是无关紧要的？不用谢!。桌面Linux仍然令人敬畏。
德克·霍恩德尔(Dirk Hohndel)当时是英特尔首席Linux和开源技术专家，他预测Linux将在1999年渗透到PC桌面市场，取代Windows。他创造了“Linux桌面之年”这个词。
二十年过去了，我们还在等待。大约每一年，都会有一位行业专家冒着风险宣布那一年是Linux桌面之年。这就是没有发生。大约2%的台式电脑和笔记本电脑使用Linux，2015年有超过20亿人在使用。这大约是400万台运行Linux的计算机。当然，现在的数字会更高-可能约为450万，大致相当于科威特的人口。
然而，Linux运行着世界：超过70%的网站运行在Linux上，超过92%的运行在亚马逊EC2平台上的服务器使用Linux。世界上最快的500台超级计算机都运行Linux。你的智能手机中甚至有Linux或FreeBSD派生的内核，无论它运行的是iOS还是Android，并且有超过25亿部Android智能手机。
您家中的任何智能设备几乎肯定都运行嵌入式Linux以及网络交换机、路由器和无线接入点。
我经常在活动上发言，之后，我会和很多人谈论技术。对于大多数人甚至没有听说过Linux，也不知道或不在乎操作系统是什么，我不再感到惊讶。“我遇到过一些Mac用户，他们认为自己的电脑以”苹果方式“运行Windows，还有Chromebook用户，他们认为自己使用的是Android系统。当然，不管怎么说，Android和Chrome OS都是建立在Linux内核之上的。
似乎大多数人-即使是那些参加科技演讲的人-都不会花片刻的时间去思考他们的电脑里装的是什么。他们想要时尚的硬件、快速的性能和更长的电池续航时间。最重要的是，他们希望能够运行与朋友相同的软件，或者运行他们在工作中使用的软件。
他们要么购买一台电脑或笔记本电脑，默认安装Windows，要么购买Mac电脑。人们告诉我，他们买Mac是因为他们被告知“使用起来更简单”，或者是因为他们喜欢他们的iPhone，所以他们买了一台苹果电脑。人们买电脑就像买微波炉一样。他们不在乎是什么驱使他们去，他们只想重新加热食物。
当然，还有其他更熟悉技术的人。他们可以对他们购买或自行组装的设备做出明智的决定，但他们只占少数。使用Linux桌面的人属于这一类，但我们是少数。
当我与对操作系统有更多了解的人交谈时，他们给出的不运行Linux的原因是以下一组主题的变体：
游戏：Linux上的游戏在最近几年有了突飞猛进的发展，但在Windows世界里仍然有更多的选择。认为在Windows上运行高端、优化的游戏硬件更容易的看法也依然存在。
Linux不会运行某种软件：无论是Photoshop、AutoCAD，还是微软Office，这对许多人来说都是一个巨大的交易破坏者。他们对替代方案不感兴趣，对在Wine下经营也不感兴趣。
害怕命令行：他们不想学习新的东西。
他们不想与众不同：他们想要使用他们的朋友和家人使用的相同的东西。
他们对个人自由没有意见：比如自由软件和开源。
他们不想修修补补：他们只想继续他们的工作。
戴尔和惠普分别从2007年和2004年开始提供预装Linux的硬件选项。自2005年以来，System76一直在提供专为Linux设计的高端笔记本电脑和计算机。现在出现了以Linux为操作系统的新一波计算机硬件，比如Tuxedo的一系列功能强大的机器和Pine64的经济实惠的系列。
但是谁在买这些设备呢？他们是在赢得新的改革者，还是在向现有的Linux基础销售？对于国内人群来说，很难想象除了后者之外还有其他什么，尽管企业和其他组织也可能对他们进行投资。
慕尼黑在2004年从Windows切换到Linux是出了名的，同样著名的是，10年后又换回来了。他们现在正在恢复到Linux的过程中。
巴塞罗那目前正在从Windows过渡到Linux。他们正在用开源的替代方案取代所有的桌面应用程序。当唯一使用的专有软件是操作系统时，他们会转而使用Linux。我们的想法是，员工已经熟悉了这些应用程序，所以跳到Linux不会带来太大的文化冲击。
在工作中使用Linux可能会改变人们回家后感觉舒适的跑步方式，但当然，这可以通过两种方式之一实现。
在中国，由于美国对华为的贸易禁运，我们可能很快就会看到使用Linux桌面的人数大幅增加。北京已下令其所有政府和公共机构更换所有从中国境外采购的计算机设备和软件，包括Windows。
他们还创建了自己的基于Deepin的Linux发行版来帮助他们实现这一点，称为Unity Operating System(UOS)。
如果世界运行在Linux上，那么世界需要Linux开发人员。Linux上免费提供的丰富的开发工具令人震惊。无论是应用程序、Web、跨平台还是嵌入式开发，专业质量的工具链都触手可及。如此之多，以至于开发人员纷纷离开Windows平台，转而追求卓越的Linux开发体验。
Windows Subsystem for Linux(WSL)是微软吸引他们回来的方式。最新版本WSL2包含一个真正的Linux内核，为开发人员提供了近乎裸露的Linux体验。
正如Kinks乐队的雷·戴维斯(Ray Davies)所唱的那样，“这是一个混乱、混乱、震撼的世界。”Linux在Windows10中，微软已经发布了Linux应用程序。这是我从未想过我会打字的句子。
您可以在Linux上安装微软的Visual Studio Code集成开发环境，免费用于私人和商业项目。您还可以在编写代码时使用Microsoft Teams的原生Linux客户端程序与您的同事进行协作。
这些应用程序不是开源的，所以它们是免费的(就像在啤酒中一样，而不是在演讲中)。尽管如此，微软的一些人仍然在关注这一问题。如果世界在Linux上运行，那么世界需要Linux开发人员，而微软希望他们在Windows上开发跨平台。
Linux没有接管桌面世界，但这真的很重要吗？毕竟，这是普遍存在的。它实际上已经在运营互联网和物联网了。它在太空，核潜艇和自动驾驶汽车。这是一个令人惊叹的操作系统，有一个令人惊叹的故事。
戴夫·麦凯(Dave McKay)第一次使用电脑是在打孔纸带流行的时候，从那时起，他就一直在编程。在IT行业工作了30多年后，他现在是一名全职科技记者。在他的职业生涯中，他担任过自由职业程序员、国际软件开发团队经理、IT服务项目经理，最近还担任过数据保护官。Dave是Linux的布道者和开源倡导者。阅读完整的传记»
note_en:
The fabled “Year of the Linux Desktop” has never materialized, and it likely never will. Does that mean Linux on a desktop PC is irrelevant? Not at all! Desktop Linux is still awesome.
     Dirk Hohndel, who was then the chief Linux and open-source technologist at Intel, predicted that in 1999, Linux would penetrate the PC desktop market and displace Windows. He’s credited with coining the phrase “the year of the Linux desktop.”
 Two decades later, we’re still waiting. Every year or so, an industry pundit will stick their neck out and declare that year  the year of the Linux desktop. It’s just not happening. About  two percent of desktop PCs and laptops use Linux, and there were over  2 billion in use in 2015. That’s about 4 million computers running Linux. The figure would be higher now, of course—possibly about 4.5 million, which is, roughly, the population of Kuwait.
 Yet, Linux runs the world: over  70 percent of websites run on it, and over 92 percent of the servers running on  Amazon’s EC2 platform use Linux. All 500 of the  fastest supercomputers in the world run Linux. There’s even a Linux or  FreeBSD-derived kernel in your smartphone, whether it runs iOS or Android, and there are over  2.5 billion Android smartphones.
 Any smart gadgets in your home almost certainly run embedded Linux and network switches, routers, and wireless access points.
    I frequently speak at events, and, afterward, I get to talk to a lot of people about technology. I’m no longer surprised that most people haven’t even heard of Linux, nor do they know or care what an operating system is. I’ve met Mac owners who thought their computer was running Windows “the Apple way,” and Chromebook owners who thought they were using Android. Of course, both Android and Chrome OS are built atop a Linux kernel, anyway.
 It seems the majority of people—even those at a tech talk—don’t give a moment’s thought to what is inside their computer. They want sleek hardware, fast performance, and long battery life. Above all, they want to be able to run either the same software as their friends or what they use at work.
 They either buy a computer or laptop and get Windows by default, or they buy a Mac. People tell me they bought a Mac because they were told it was “simpler to use,” or because they like their iPhone, so they got an Apple computer. People buy computers like they buy microwaves. They don’t care what makes them go, they just want to reheat food.
 Of course, there are others who are more familiar with technology. They can make informed decisions about the equipment they buy or self-assemble, but they’re in the minority. People who use Linux desktops are in this category, but we’re the minority of the minority.
 When I speak to people who understand a bit more about operating systems, the reasons they give for not running Linux are variations on a set of the following themes:
 Gaming: Gaming on Linux has improved in leaps and bounds over recent years, but there’s still more choice in the Windows world. The perception that it’s easier to run top-end, optimized gaming hardware on Windows also persists.
 Linux won’t run a certain software: Whether it’s  Photoshop,  AutoCAD, or  Microsoft Office, this is a deal-breaker for many people. They’re not interested in alternatives or running things under  Wine.
 Fear of the command line: They don’t want to have to learn something new.
 They don’t want to be different: They want to use the same things their friends and family use.
 They don’t have an opinion on personal freedoms: Such as free software and open source.
 They don’t want to tinker: They just want to get on with their work.
   Dell and  HP have offered hardware options pre-installed with Linux since 2007 and 2004, respectively.  System76 has been providing designed-for-Linux, high-end laptops and computers since 2005. There’s a new wave of computer hardware shipping with Linux as the operating system, like the range of powerful machines from  Tuxedo and the budget-friendly line from  Pine64.
 But who’s buying these devices? Are they winning new converts or selling to the established Linux base? It’s difficult to imagine anything other than the latter for the domestic crowd, although businesses and other organizations might also invest in them.
 The city of Munich famously switched from Windows to Linux in 2004 and, equally famously, swapped back 10 years later. They’re now in the process of reverting to Linux.
 Barcelona’s transition from Windows to Linux is currently underway. They’re replacing all desktop applications with open-source alternatives. They’ll swap over to Linux when the only proprietary software in use is the operating system. The idea is the staff will already be familiar with the applications, so the hop to Linux won’t be much of a culture shock.
 Using Linux at work might change what people feel comfortable running when they get home, but that can go in one of two ways, of course.
  In China, we might soon see a large spike in the number of people using Linux desktops as a result of the  U.S. trade embargo with  Huawei. Beijing has ordered every one of its government and public institutions  to replace all computer equipment and software sourced from outside China, including Windows.
 They’ve also created their own  Deepin-based Linux distribution to help them achieve this, called the  Unity Operating System (UOS).
    If the world runs on Linux, the world needs Linux developers. The wealth of development tools that are freely available on Linux is astounding. Whether for application, web, cross-platform, or embedded development, professional-quality toolchains are at your fingertips. So much so, that developers were leaving the Windows platform for the superior Linux development experience.
 The  Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL) was Microsoft’s way of luring them back. The latest version, WSL2, includes a genuine Linux kernel, providing a close to naked Linux experience for the developer.
 As Ray Davies of The Kinks sings, “It’s a mixed up, muddled up, shook up world.” Linux is inside Windows 10, and Microsoft has released applications for Linux. That’s a sentence I never thought I’d type.
 You can install Microsoft’s  Visual Studio Code integrated development environment on Linux and freely use it for private and commercial projects. You can also use a Microsoft Teams  native Linux client to collaborate with your colleagues while you’re coding.
 These applications are not open source, so they’re free (as in beer, not as in speech). Still, someone at Microsoft is sitting up and taking notice. If the world runs on Linux, the world needs Linux developers, and Microsoft wants them to be developing cross-platform on Windows.
  Linux didn’t take over the desktop world, but does that really matter? After all, it’s universal. It already practically runs the Internet and the  Internet of Things. It’s in space, nuclear submarines, and self-driving cars. It’s an amazing operating system with an amazing story.
 Dave McKay  Dave McKay first used computers when punched paper tape was in vogue, and he has been programming ever since. After over 30 years in the IT industry, he is now a full-time technology journalist. During his career, he has worked as a freelance programmer, manager of an international software development team, an IT services project manager, and, most recently, as a Data Protection Officer. Dave is a Linux evangelist and open source advocate.   Read Full Bio »
title: 为什么桌面Linux仍然重要

note:
特朗普任命的美国联邦媒体机构新任局长解雇了三名高级官员，这引发了人们对网络将被政治化的担忧。
保守派电影制片人、前白宫顾问史蒂夫·班农(Steve Bannon)的前同事迈克尔·帕克(Michael Pack)也开始在该机构安插忠于特朗普的人。
帕克为自己的行为辩护，称这是任何新的组织领导人都会采取的过渡步骤。
美国全球媒体署(USAGM)是一个由纳税人出资的机构，负责监督包括美国之音、自由欧洲电台/自由电台、自由亚洲电台和中东广播在内的全球广播公司。
USAGM的任务是向全球超过2.8亿人广播独立新闻。它创建于1942年，目的是打击纳粹的宣传。
周一，在帕克获得参议院确认后，美国之音总监阿曼达·班尼特(Amanda Bennett)和她的副手桑迪·杉原(Sandy Sugawa)辞职。
周三，自由欧洲电台、亚洲电台、中东广播电台以及开放技术基金的负责人被赶下台。
美国总统唐纳德·特朗普(Donald Trump)最近批评了美国之音，称其报道令人厌恶，并指责其散布有利于中国的宣传。
自由欧洲电台(Radio Free Europe)、自由电台(Radio Liberty)和中东广播网的领导人--杰米·弗莱(Jamie Fly)和阿尔贝托·费尔南德斯(Alberto Fernandez)--都是在特朗普担任总统期间被任命的。
弗莱曾是共和党参议员马尔科·鲁比奥(Marco Rubio)的顾问，而费尔南德斯则是前大使。
一些长期担任顾问委员会成员的人也被免职，帕克现在是这些机构董事会的主席。
据CNBC报道，帕克还任命了一些前特朗普政府官员-包括来自司法部、国土安全部和管理和预算办公室的官员-担任该机构的高级职位。
民主党人尤其关注乔纳森·亚历山大(Jonathan Alexandre)加入自由亚洲电台(Radio Free Asia)董事会一事，因为他是自由顾问(Liberty Counsel)的政策主管，自由顾问因反对LGBT权利而被南方贫困法律中心指定为仇恨团体。
在被解雇之前，帕克向员工发送了一封内部电子邮件，称他将保持该机构的独立性。
他后来告诉员工，没有新领导人的明确批准，不能采取任何行动，也不能进行外部沟通。
帕克表示：我采取的每一项行动都是--我将采取的每一项行动--都是为了重建USAGM的声誉，鼓舞士气，改善内容。
这个计划完全是根据国会和上届政府制定和批准的两党语言制定的，这两个政府都很容易认识到，该机构已经变得过时，无法有效地履行其法定使命。
资深外交关系委员会民主党参议员鲍勃·梅嫩德斯(Bob Menendez)称，大规模解雇该网络负责人是对该组织历史和使命的严重破坏。
可悲的是，这次攻击是特朗普政府将植根于民主原则的美国机构转变为总统个人议程工具的最新努力，但不是最后一次。
民主党人、众议院外交事务委员会(House Foreign Affairs Committee)主席艾略特·恩格尔(Eliot Engel)和众议院拨款委员会主席尼塔·洛维(Nita Lowey)周四表示，他们对帕克优先考虑政治突发奇想的行为感到愤怒。
前国家安全顾问、财政部助理部长、共和党人胡安·萨拉特(Juan Zarate)表示，弗莱和费尔南德斯被免职令人费解。
note_en:
The new chief of the US federal media agency, a Trump-appointee, has sacked three senior officials, raising fears that networks will be politicised.
 Michael Pack, a conservative filmmaker and former associate of ex-White House adviser Steve Bannon, has also begun to install Trump loyalists at the agency.
  Mr Pack has defended his actions as transitional steps that any new leader of an organisation would take.
 The US Agency for Global Media (USAGM) is a taxpayer funded body that oversees global broadcasters including Voice of America, Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty, Radio Free Asia and Middle East Broadcasting.
  The USAGM is tasked with broadcasting independent news to a global audience of more than 280 million people. It was created in 1942 to combat Nazi propaganda.
  The director of Voice of America, Amanda Bennett, and her deputy Sandy Sugawara resigned on Monday after Mr Pack was confirmed by the Senate.
 The heads of Radio Free Europe, Asia, Middle East Broadcasting as well as the Open Technology Fund were ousted on Wednesday.
  President Donald Trump has recently criticised Voice of America, calling its reporting "disgusting" and accusing it of spreading "propaganda" in favour of China.
 The leaders of the Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty and Middle East Broadcasting networks - Jamie Fly and Alberto Fernandez - were both appointed during Mr Trump's presidency.
 Mr Fly was a former adviser to Republican Senator Marco Rubio while Mr Fernandez was a former ambassador.
 A number of long-time advisory board members have also been removed, and Mr Pack is now the chairman of these outlets' boards of directors.
 Mr Pack has also appointed a number of former Trump administration officials - including from the Departments of Justice, Homeland Security and Office of Management and Budget - to high level positions in the agency, CNBC reported.
  Democrats have been particularly concerned by Jonathan Alexandre's addition to the board of Radio Free Asia, as he is a policy director for the Liberty Counsel, which has been designated as a hate group by the Southern Poverty Law Center for its opposition to LGBT rights.
 The sackings followed an internal email to staff from Mr Pack, saying he would maintain "the agency's independence".
 He later told staff "no actions are to be taken, and no external communications are to be made without explicit approval" from the new leaders.
   "Every action I carried out was - and every action I will carry out will be - geared toward rebuilding the USAGM's reputation, boosting morale, and improving content," Mr Pack said.
 "This plan is wholly pursuant to the bipartisan language that was developed and approved by Congress and the previous administration, both of which readily recognised that the agency had grown obsolete and ineffective implementing its statutory mission."
 Ranking Foreign Relations Committee Democratic Senator Bob Menendez called the "wholesale firing" of the network heads an "egregious breach of this organisation's history and mission".
 "This attack is sadly the latest - but not the last - in the Trump administration's efforts to transform US institutions rooted in the principles of democracy into tools for the president's own personal agenda."
 Democrats Eliot Engel, chairman of the House of Representatives Foreign Affairs Committee, and Nita Lowey, chairwoman of the House Appropriation Committee, said on Thursday they were  "outraged" by Mr Pack's actions that prioritised "political whims".
 Former National Security adviser and Treasury Assistant Secretary Juan Zarate, a Republican, said the removals of Mr Fly and Mr Fernandez were "incomprehensible".
title: 特朗普任命的首席编辑被解雇，人们对美国媒体独立性的担忧
title_en: Fears over US media independence as Trump-appointed chief fires editors

note:
言论自由通常被认为是运作良好的民主的关键。在许多国家，言论自由被认为是比常规选举更重要的民主价值。但是，通过支持每个社会群体的言论自由，人们真的相信公开辩论的好处吗？或者他们只支持他们自己的群体的言论自由？在最近发表在“社会心理学和人格科学”上的一篇论文中，我们旨在回答这些问题，并试图探索更高的认知能力是否与更有原则的言论自由立场有关。我们预计认知能力较高的人会更倾向于接受开放的思想交流，在这种交流中，观点可以被仔细审查和挑战，以培养知情的决策和知识。因此，假设认知能力与更广泛的言论自由有关，所有社会群体都可以在不同的意识形态范围内获得更广泛的言论支持。
在第一项研究中，我们分析了综合社会调查在1974年至2018年期间收集的21波数据。结果显示，认知能力较高的人更倾向于言论自由，这一模式在40年的数据收集过程中是稳定的。更准确地说，认知能力测试的较高分数与支持调查中所有社会群体的言论自由有关，这些群体代表了不同的意识形态立场(例如社会主义者、军国主义者、种族主义者、反宗教主义者)。表1描述了意识形态光谱相对相反的两个模范群体(军国主义者和反宗教主义者)的结果。
表1.军国主义者(左)和反宗教者(右)的认知能力与言论自由支持之间的关系。
注意。结果表示优势比(OR)。OR&lt；1表明认知能力水平越高，对言论自由的支持越弱，而OR&gt；1表示认知能力水平越高，对言论自由的支持越强。如图所示，结果显示所有年份的OR&gt；1。
在第二项研究中，我们对更广泛的社会群体的认知能力和言论自由支持之间的关系进行了调查。我们还包括了对这些社会群体的情感偏见的衡量，以及对这些群体意识形态的衡量。与之前的研究一致，较高的认知能力与喜欢“自由派”群体(如社会主义者、同性恋者、反宗教人士)和不喜欢“保守”群体(如军队、大企业、茶党、基督教原教旨主义者)有关。然而，与第一项研究一样，更高的认知能力与更强烈地支持所有社会群体的言论自由权利有关。因此，认知能力较高的人更强烈地支持全面的言论自由，即使是他们相对不喜欢的社会群体也是如此。
表2。研究2中不同目标群体的认知能力与言论自由支持之间的关系。
注意。结果表示相关性(R)。r&lt；0表示认知能力水平越高，对言论自由的支持越弱，而r&lt；0表示认知能力水平越高，对言论自由的支持越强。如图所示，结果显示所有组的r&gt；0。
在第三项也是最后一项研究中，我们再次复制了认知能力与支持所有不同意识形态群体言论自由之间的积极关系。此外，根据我们预先登记的假设，我们发现了更高水平的智力谦逊可以解释认知能力和言论自由支持之间的关系的证据。换句话说，认知能力较高的人可能会支持言论自由的原则，因为他们更独立于智力和自我，开放地修改自己的观点，尊重他人的观点，并且没有智力上的过度自信。
这一系列研究表明，认知能力与支持不同意识形态群体的言论自由有关。这些结果并不意味着认知能力较高的人是言论自由绝对主义者。事实上，虽然认知能力可靠地与每个社会群体相对较强的言论自由支持有关，但宣扬仇恨和暴力的群体(例如种族主义者、反美穆斯林神职人员)得到的言论自由支持的绝对值相当有限。然而，结果确实表明，认知能力较高的个人更欣赏意识形态光谱上不同地方的群体对不同观点的自由流动。事实上，即使这些团体表达了他们不喜欢的想法。
所有HxA博客内容均采用知识共享署名、非商业性、无衍生4.0国际许可。请在此处查看我们的辛迪加指南。
note_en:
Freedom of speech is often considered key to a well-functional democracy. In many countries, freedom of speech is considered a  more important democratic value than regular elections. But do people genuinely believe in the virtues of open debates by supporting freedom of speech for  every social group? Or do they support free speech only for their own groups? In a  recently published paper in Social Psychological and Personality Science, we aimed to answer these questions, and we sought to explore whether higher cognitive ability was associated with more principled positions on free speech. We expected that people with higher cognitive abilities would be more inclined to embrace the open exchange of ideas, wherein viewpoints can be scrutinized and challenged in order to foster informed decision making and knowledge. Therefore, it was hypothesized that cognitive ability is related to more generalized freedom of speech support for all social groups across the ideological spectrum.
 In a first study, we analyzed 21 waves of data collected by the General Social Survey between 1974 and 2018. Results showed that individuals with higher cognitive ability were more in favor of free speech, a pattern that was stable across four decades of data collection. More precisely, higher scores on a cognitive ability test were related to supporting freedom of speech for all social groups included in the survey, representing a variety of ideological stances (e.g., socialists, militarists, racists, anti-religionists). Table 1 depicts the results for two exemplary groups at relatively opposite ends of the ideological spectrum (militarists and anti-religionists).
 Table 1. The relationship between cognitive ability and freedom of speech support by year for militarists (left) and anti-religionists (right).
  Note. Results represent odds ratio’s (OR). An OR < 1 indicates that higher levels of cognitive ability are related to weaker support for free speech, whereas OR > 1 indicates that higher levels of cognitive ability are related to stronger support for free speech. As depicted, the results show OR > 1 for all years.
 In a second study, we examined the relationship between cognitive ability and freedom of speech support for a broader range of social groups. We also included a measure of affective prejudice towards these social groups and measured perceptions of these groups’ ideology. Consistent with previous research, higher cognitive ability was associated with liking ‘liberal’ groups (e.g., socialists, homosexuals, anti-religionists) and disliking ‘conservative’ groups (e.g., military, big business, tea party, Christian fundamentalists). Nevertheless, as in the first study, higher cognitive ability was related to more strongly endorsing free speech rights for   all social groups. Hence, people with higher levels of cognitive ability more strongly support freedom of speech across the board, even for social groups they relatively dislike.
 Table 2. The relationship between cognitive ability and freedom of speech support for various target groups in Study 2.
  Note. Results represent correlations (r). An r < 0 indicates that higher levels of cognitive ability are related to weaker support for free speech, whereas an r  > 0 indicates that higher levels of cognitive ability are related to stronger support for free speech. As depicted, the results show r > 0 for all groups.
 In a third and final study, we again replicated the positive relationship between cognitive ability and supporting freedom of speech for all groups across the ideological spectrum. Moreover, in line with our pre-registered hypothesis, we found evidence that higher levels of Intellectual Humility could explain the relationship between cognitive ability and free speech support. In other words, those with higher cognitive ability might support principles of free speech because of their greater independence of intellect and ego, openness to revise their viewpoints, respect for others’ viewpoints, and lack of intellectual overconfidence.
 The series of studies suggest that cognitive ability is related to support for freedom of speech for groups across the ideological spectrum. These results do not mean that people with higher cognitive abilities are free speech absolutists. Indeed, although cognitive ability was reliably related to relatively stronger free speech support for each social group, groups that preach hate and violence (e.g., racists, anti-American Muslim clergymen) received rather limited freedom of speech support in absolute terms. The results do suggest, however, that individuals with higher cognitive ability are more appreciative of the free flow of divergent ideas by groups at various places on the ideological spectrum. Indeed, even when these groups voice ideas that they don’t like.
